label: ai
topic: A Chinese-American adoptee's relationship with dumplings as identity
text:
The first dumplings I remember were frozen, from a plastic bag my mother bought at the grocery store in Glendale, Ohio. Glendale had a population of two thousand, one traffic light, and no Chinese restaurants. My mother, who was from Cincinnati and had never been to China, boiled the dumplings in a pot she usually used for corn on the cob and served them with soy sauce she had driven twenty minutes to buy.

She was trying. She had adopted a baby from Changsha and brought her home to a town where the closest thing to Chinese culture was the placemat zodiac at the Hunan Palace in the next county. She bought children's books about the Lunar New Year. She hung a red paper lantern in my bedroom.

The dumplings were fine. Soft and slightly bland. I ate them and did not think about what they were supposed to taste like, because I had no original to compare them to.

In elementary school, I brought leftover dumplings to the cafeteria. A boy named Garrett said they looked weird. I put them back and ate the granola bar my mother had packed as backup, which she always packed, as if she knew. After that, I asked for sandwiches.

I did not think about dumplings again until high school, when our AP History class visited Chicago. I wandered into a restaurant in Chinatown with red vinyl booths and fluorescent lights. I ordered pork dumplings because the menu said jiaozi and that word felt like it should mean something to me.

They arrived in a bamboo steamer, twelve of them, pleated with a precision like origami. The skin was thin and slippery and the filling was ginger and scallion and pork, and the taste was so different from my mother's grocery-store version that I understood, for the first time, the distance between the thing and its imitation.

I cried in the restaurant. My friend Danielle asked if I was okay. I said the ginger was strong, which was true but not the reason.

What I could not articulate then is the grief of encountering something that should have been yours and recognising it as a stranger. Those dumplings were familiar the way a song you have never heard can be familiar, as if it were playing in a room you passed through as an infant. I understood there was a version of my life in which jiaozi were ordinary, in which I would have learned to pleat them at a grandmother's table. That version does not exist, and mourning it feels ungrateful. My mother drove twenty minutes for soy sauce. The imperfect connection was not her failure but the nature of adoption: a bridge over a gap that cannot be fully closed.

I learned to make dumplings in college. My roommate's mother, from Sichuan, taught me. She handed me dough and showed me how to fold it, pinching edges with a speed that suggested ten thousand repetitions. My dumplings were ugly. Hers were beautiful. She said mine would get better.

I made them for my mother over winter break. She bit into one and said, "These are so much better than the bag." She asked me to show her how. We stood at the counter, her hands floury, mine guiding hers, and I taught my mother to make jiaozi in a kitchen in Glendale, Ohio.

I make dumplings now with the regularity of a private ritual. The pleats are not perfect. They are mine. I do not know what was lost when I was carried from Changsha to Ohio. But I can fold dough around filling and eat something that tastes like it belongs to me, even if I had to learn it as a foreign language, even if I will always speak it with an accent.
